summary: Joel provides brief updates on several technical topics he's been discussing. He addresses VNC alternatives, noting TightVNC is faster but still inferior to Terminal Services. He shares data on .NET CLR browser penetration rates (6-8%, lower on consumer sites). He also discusses RSS bandwidth waste, concluding that HTTP spec already has the solution and aggregators need to implement it, praising Dave Winer for quick implementation and calling on others to follow.
key_points: TightVNC improves on VNC but still falls far short of Terminal Services in speed and usability
.NET CLR penetration was only 6-8% in server logs, with consumer-oriented sites seeing as low as 2-3%
RSS bandwidth problems could be solved by properly implementing existing HTTP spec features rather than inventing new solutions
Open source and indie developers responded quickly to the bandwidth concern, with Radio Userland and NetNewsWire fixing the issue within days
money_quotes: It's faster than VNC but nowhere near as fast and usable as Terminal Services.
The conclusion is the best way to reduce this bandwidth is already a part of the HTTP spec, and needs to be implemented by the aggregators.
I don't want to have to hide the feed from bandwidth hogging user agents.
I shall now shut up about this topic because I know it's excruciatingly boring to the rest of the world.
key_insight: The RSS bandwidth problem didn't need a new protocol or standard — the solution already existed in HTTP and just needed aggregator developers to implement it properly.
